g.data-narration: Welcome to Combinational Circuits - the foundation of digital logic that processes data without memory.
A combinational circuit outputs depend only on current inputs - no memory, no history, just pure logic computation.
g.text: A digital circuit where output depends
ONLY on present inputs - no memory or feedback.

g.data-narration: The block diagram shows inputs flowing through combinational logic to produce outputs - a one-way data pipeline.
Four key properties define combinational circuits: no memory, no feedback, instant response, and Boolean function representation.
Analyzing a circuit involves labeling gate outputs, writing Boolean expressions, combining them, and building a truth table.
g.text: Analysis Process: How to Understand a Circuit

g.data-narration: Let's analyze: Gate 1 computes T1 = A·B, Gate 2 computes T2 = B·C, then F = T1 + T2 = AB + BC = B(A + C).
Synthesis reverses analysis: define inputs/outputs, create truth table, simplify with Boolean algebra or K-maps, then draw the circuit.
Data processing circuits perform arithmetic: Adders sum binary numbers, Comparators evaluate magnitude, and ALUs combine operations.
g.text: Types of Combinational Circuits - Data Processing

g.data-narration: Data transfer circuits route information: Multiplexers select one input to pass, while Demultiplexers direct one input to multiple outputs.
Code conversion circuits transform between binary representations: Encoders compress inputs, Decoders expand codes, and specialized converters handle BCD, Gray code.
Two-level circuits are faster with two gate delays; multi-level circuits use factoring to reduce gates but add delay - a speed-versus-cost tradeoff.
g.text: Direct SOP/POS • Max 2 gate delays • Faster
Intermediate gates • Fewer gates possible • Slower
F = A(B + CD) is 3 levels, not 2

g.data-narration: Real circuits have propagation delay causing glitches. The critical path is the longest input-to-output delay, determining circuit speed.
Remember: No memory means output depends only on current inputs. Each output is a Boolean function. Analysis understands circuits, synthesis creates them.
g.text: 1. No memory - output depends ONLY on current inputs
2. Each output is a Boolean function of inputs
3. Analysis → Understand circuit | Synthesis → Create circuit

g.data-narration: Combinational circuits are everywhere: calculators, network routing, CPU instruction decoding, and keyboard encoding - the building blocks of all digital devices.
